title: Ask for a Job Reference
summary: Request that a teacher, former employer, or mentor serve as a professional reference in a polite and respectful way.
learnerPromise: You will be able to ask someone to be your job reference in a way that feels comfortable for both of you and gives them the information they need.
whyItMatters: Most employers want to hear from people who know your work. A polite request builds trust with your reference and makes them more likely to speak well of you.
realLifeUses: Asking a former teacher to vouch for your reliability when applying for your first job
Requesting a reference from a supervisor at a volunteer organization
Asking a previous manager to serve as a reference for a new position
Contacting a family friend who hired you for informal work to ask if you can list them
materialsNeeded: Phone or email to contact the person
A short list of jobs you are applying for
Your resume or a brief summary of your recent activities
result: A confirmed reference who has agreed and knows what to expect.
youWillLearn: How to choose the right person to ask based on your relationship and the job you want
How to make the request politely by email, message, or in person
How to give your reference helpful context about the jobs you are applying for
How to follow up with a thank-you and keep your references informed
miniChallenge: Choose one person who knows your work well, prepare what you want to say, and ask them if they would be willing to be a reference for you.
steps: Choose someone who knows your work ethic well: a teacher, coach, former boss, volunteer supervisor, or client.
Contact them privately. A brief email or message is often best. Start by asking how they are doing.
Politely ask if they would be comfortable being a reference for you. Mention the type of jobs you are applying for.
If they say yes, give them a copy of your resume and a short summary of your skills and recent activities.
Thank them sincerely. Let them know you will tell them before sharing their contact information with employers.
completionCriteria: You have at least one person who has agreed to be a reference for you
Your reference has your current resume and knows what kinds of jobs you are applying for
commonProblems: Asking someone who does not know your work well — choose people who can speak specifically about you
Forgetting to warn your reference that a call might be coming — always give them a heads-up
Asking at the last minute, which can feel disrespectful — ask at least a few days in advance
Not updating references when you no longer need them, leading to unexpected calls years later
tips: It is okay to ask even if you have no formal job experience — teachers and volunteer supervisors make great references
Always ask permission before listing someone's phone number or email on an application
Keep a list of three to five references so you have options for different types of jobs
Send a thank-you note when you get the job, and let your references know the outcome
